content.text: Azure Arc-enabled PostgreSQL deploys a Citus-powered PostgreSQL cluster inside a Kubernetes cluster on any infrastructure. Unlike Arc SQL MI (which is a single SQL Server pod or AG), Arc PostgreSQL is a distributed, horizontally scalable database: a coordinator node routes queries and metadata while multiple worker nodes each hold a subset of table shards. This architecture allows scale-out read and write performance for time-series, IoT, and analytics workloads.

attrs.alt: Arc PostgreSQL — coordinator, workers, Kubernetes services, Azure ARM

content.text: Arc PostgreSQL is built on Citus, the open-source PostgreSQL extension for sharding and distributed query execution. Applications connect to the coordinator only on port 5432 using standard PostgreSQL drivers — the distribution is transparent to the client.
Arc PostgreSQL vs Other Arc Data Services
SQL Server license + Azure Hybrid Benefit

Arc PostgreSQL requires the Arc Data Controller to be running first. Specify the number of worker nodes at creation time; workers can be scaled up later but cannot be reduced below the initial count without re-deploying.

# Ensure the Arc Data Controller is running
kubectl get pods -n arc-data-services | grep Running

# Deploy Arc PostgreSQL with 3 worker nodes
az postgres arc-server create   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --workers 3   --cores-request 2   --cores-limit 4   --memory-request 4Gi   --memory-limit 8Gi   --storage-class-data managed-premium   --storage-class-logs managed-premium   --volume-size-data 100Gi   --volume-size-logs 10Gi

# Get the coordinator external endpoint
az postgres arc-server show   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "status.primaryEndpoint"

# Connect using psql (coordinator only)
ENDPOINT=$(az postgres arc-server show   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "status.primaryEndpoint" -o tsv)
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d postgres

Citus distributes tables by a distribution column using hash sharding. Choose a column with high cardinality and that appears in most query WHERE clauses — this determines co-location and join efficiency. The coordinator stores shard placement metadata in pg_dist_shard and pg_dist_placement.

Add workers to expand storage capacity and query parallelism. After adding workers, rebalance the shards to distribute existing data to the new nodes — this is done via the rebalance_table_shards() Citus function and runs incrementally without taking the table offline.

# Add a worker node (scales the StatefulSet)
az postgres arc-server update   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --workers 5

# Wait for new worker pods to be Running
kubectl get pods -n arc-data-services -l app=pg-arc01 --watch

# Verify Citus sees the new workers
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d postgres   -c "SELECT * FROM citus_get_active_worker_nodes();"

# Rebalance shards to include new workers (non-blocking, runs as background tasks)
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d postgres   -c "SELECT rebalance_table_shards();"

# Monitor rebalance progress
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d postgres   -c "SELECT * FROM get_rebalance_progress();"

# After rebalance completes, verify even shard distribution
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d postgres   -c "SELECT nodename, count(*) AS shard_count FROM citus_shards GROUP BY nodename ORDER BY 1;"

Arc PostgreSQL uses pg_basebackup internally for automated full backups and WAL archiving for point-in-time recovery. Backups land on the backup PVC configured at deployment. PITR window depends on the retention setting; the default is 7 days.

# Show current backup configuration
az postgres arc-server show   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --query "{retention:spec.backup.retentionPeriodInDays}"

# Update retention to 14 days
az postgres arc-server update   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --retention-days 14

# Point-in-time restore to a new instance
az postgres arc-server restore   --name pg-arc01   --dest-name pg-arc01-restore   --time "2024-03-15T10:30:00Z"   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# List available restore times
az postgres arc-server list-restore-windows   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s

# Manual pg_dump on the coordinator (app-level logical backup)
kubectl exec -n arc-data-services pg-arc01-coordinator-0 --   pg_dump -U postgres -Fc -f /tmp/mydb.dump mydb

kubectl cp arc-data-services/pg-arc01-coordinator-0:/tmp/mydb.dump ./mydb.dump
Connecting directly to a worker node
Always connect to the coordinator endpoint
Joining distributed tables on non-distribution column
Query fans out to all shards (slow)
Redesign distribution column or use co-location
Duplicate key errors on distributed inserts
Use gen_random_uuid() or bigserial with Citus sequences
Insufficient shard count for future scale
Default 32 shards/table; increase with citus.shard_count before create_distributed_table
Geospatial functions fail on worker shards
CREATE EXTENSION postgis on coordinator; Citus propagates to workers
Backup PVC too small for WAL volume
PITR restore fails: WAL segments missing
Size backup PVC ≥ 3× peak daily WAL volume
PostGIS: Geospatial Queries on Arc PostgreSQL

PostGIS is available as a PostgreSQL extension and runs across all Citus worker nodes. When the events or telemetry table is distributed by a location-based key, geospatial queries using ST_DWithin, ST_Contains, and ST_Intersects benefit from parallel shard execution. Reference tables (not distributed) are replicated to all workers so spatial join lookups stay local.

-- Enable PostGIS (coordinator propagates to all workers automatically)
CREATE EXTENSION postgis;
CREATE EXTENSION postgis_topology;

-- Geospatial distributed table (distributed by region_id for co-location)
CREATE TABLE sensor_readings (
  region_id   INT,
  sensor_id   UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  location    GEOGRAPHY(POINT, 4326),
  temperature NUMERIC,
  humidity    NUMERIC
);
SELECT create_distributed_table('sensor_readings', 'region_id');

-- Reference table: region boundaries (replicated to all workers)
CREATE TABLE regions (
  region_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        TEXT,
  boundary    GEOGRAPHY(POLYGON, 4326)
);
SELECT create_reference_table('regions');

-- Geospatial query: readings within 5 km of a point (executes in parallel on all shards)
SELECT s.sensor_id, s.recorded_at, s.temperature,
       ST_Distance(s.location, ST_GeographyFromText('POINT(-122.4194 37.7749)')) AS dist_m
FROM sensor_readings s
WHERE ST_DWithin(s.location, ST_GeographyFromText('POINT(-122.4194 37.7749)'), 5000)
  AND s.recorded_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour'
ORDER BY dist_m
LIMIT 100;

-- Readings inside a region boundary (distributed + reference join)
SELECT s.sensor_id, r.name AS region_name, AVG(s.temperature) AS avg_temp
FROM sensor_readings s
JOIN regions r ON s.region_id = r.region_id
WHERE ST_Contains(r.boundary, s.location)
  AND s.recorded_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours'
GROUP BY s.sensor_id, r.name
ORDER BY avg_temp DESC;

Arc PostgreSQL does not ship a built-in PgBouncer sidecar, but a high-concurrency application connecting to the coordinator can exhaust PostgreSQL max_connections quickly. Deploy PgBouncer as a Kubernetes Deployment in the same namespace and route application traffic through it.

# pgbouncer-config.yaml — ConfigMap for PgBouncer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: pgbouncer-config
  namespace: arc-data-services
data:
  pgbouncer.ini: |
    [databases]
    * = host=pg-arc01-coordinator-svc port=5432 dbname=postgres
    [pgbouncer]
    listen_port = 6432
    listen_addr = 0.0.0.0
    auth_type = md5
    auth_file = /etc/pgbouncer/userlist.txt
    pool_mode = transaction
    max_client_conn = 1000
    default_pool_size = 20
    min_pool_size = 5
    reserve_pool_size = 5
    server_tls_sslmode = require
    log_connections = 1
    log_disconnections = 1
  userlist.txt: |
    "appuser" "md5<hashed_password>"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: pgbouncer
  namespace: arc-data-services
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels: {app: pgbouncer}
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: {app: pgbouncer}
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: pgbouncer
        image: pgbouncer/pgbouncer:1.22.1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6432
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /etc/pgbouncer
          readOnly: true
        resources:
          requests: {cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi}
          limits:  {cpu: 500m, memory: 256Mi}
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: pgbouncer-config
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: pgbouncer-svc
  namespace: arc-data-services
spec:
  selector: {app: pgbouncer}
  ports:
  - port: 6432
    targetPort: 6432

TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension compatible with Arc PostgreSQL that adds automatic time partitioning (hypertables), continuous aggregates, and data retention policies. For IoT and monitoring workloads, hypertables combined with Citus distribution give both time-based partition pruning and horizontal shard parallelism.

-- Install TimescaleDB (on coordinator; propagated to workers)
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS timescaledb;

-- Create a hypertable (regular PostgreSQL table + timescaledb partitioning)
CREATE TABLE device_metrics (
  device_id   UUID NOT NULL,
  region_id   INT  NOT NULL,  -- used for Citus sharding
  recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  cpu_pct     NUMERIC,
  mem_pct     NUMERIC,
  disk_io_ms  NUMERIC
);

-- Convert to hypertable partitioned by time (1-hour chunks)
SELECT create_hypertable('device_metrics', 'recorded_at',
  chunk_time_interval => INTERVAL '1 hour');

-- Also distribute with Citus (region_id co-locates region queries)
SELECT create_distributed_table('device_metrics', 'region_id',
  chunk_time_interval => INTERVAL '1 hour');  -- Citus-TimescaleDB joint option

-- Continuous aggregate: pre-compute 5-minute averages
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW device_metrics_5m
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT device_id,
       region_id,
       time_bucket('5 minutes', recorded_at) AS bucket,
       AVG(cpu_pct)    AS avg_cpu,
       AVG(mem_pct)    AS avg_mem,
       MAX(disk_io_ms) AS max_disk_io
FROM device_metrics
GROUP BY device_id, region_id, bucket;

-- Automatic refresh policy (keep aggregate up to date in background)
SELECT add_continuous_aggregate_policy('device_metrics_5m',
  start_offset => INTERVAL '1 hour',
  end_offset   => INTERVAL '1 minute',
  schedule_interval => INTERVAL '5 minutes');

-- Data retention: drop chunks older than 30 days (saves storage)
SELECT add_retention_policy('device_metrics', INTERVAL '30 days');

-- Compress chunks older than 7 days (native columnar compression)
SELECT add_compression_policy('device_metrics', INTERVAL '7 days');

Arc PostgreSQL auto-generates a self-signed TLS certificate and requires SSL by default. For production, provide a CA-signed certificate. Access control is managed through pg_hba.conf, which is exposed as a Kubernetes secret and can be patched without restarting the coordinator.

# Supply a custom TLS certificate
kubectl create secret generic pg-arc01-certificate   --from-file=server.crt=./my-cert.pem   --from-file=server.key=./my-key.pem   -n arc-data-services

az postgres arc-server update   --name pg-arc01   --k8s-namespace arc-data-services   --use-k8s   --service-cert-secret pg-arc01-certificate

# View current pg_hba.conf (stored as a Kubernetes secret)
kubectl get secret pg-arc01-pg-hba-conf -n arc-data-services   -o jsonpath='{.data.pg_hba\.conf}' | base64 -d

# Add a SCRAM-SHA-256 rule for an application user
# Edit the secret directly (triggers a coordinator config reload, not a pod restart)
kubectl patch secret pg-arc01-pg-hba-conf -n arc-data-services   --type='json'   -p='[{"op":"replace","path":"/data/pg_hba.conf","value":"'"$(echo '
host all appuser 10.0.0.0/8  scram-sha-256
host all postgres 127.0.0.1/32 scram-sha-256
' | base64 -w0)"'"}]'

# Create application role with least-privilege access
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d mydb <<SQL
CREATE ROLE appuser WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'App@Password1' CONNECTION LIMIT 100;
GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE mydb TO appuser;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO appuser;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO appuser;
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public
  GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLES TO appuser;
SQL

The Arc Data Controller deploys InfluxDB and Grafana for local metrics. In indirect mode these dashboards are accessible only via port-forward. In direct mode the controller pushes PostgreSQL and Citus metrics to Azure Monitor automatically.

# Port-forward Grafana for local dashboard (indirect mode)
kubectl port-forward svc/metricsui-external-svc 3000:3000 -n arc-data-services &

# Port-forward Kibana for log exploration
kubectl port-forward svc/logsui-external-svc 5601:5601 -n arc-data-services &

# Key pg_stat_* views to check on the coordinator
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -d mydb -c "
SELECT schemaname, relname, seq_scan, idx_scan,
       n_live_tup, n_dead_tup, last_autovacuum, last_autoanalyze
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC
LIMIT 20;"

# Check replication lag (coordinator -> worker WAL)
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -c "
SELECT client_addr, state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn,
       (sent_lsn - replay_lsn) AS replication_lag_bytes
FROM pg_stat_replication;"

# Citus query performance: active queries distributed across shards
psql -h $ENDPOINT -p 5432 -U postgres -c "
SELECT query_id, execution_duration_ms, partition_key_value, task_id
FROM citus_stat_activity
WHERE state = 'active'
ORDER BY execution_duration_ms DESC
LIMIT 20;"

# Resource Graph: Arc PostgreSQL instances by worker count
# resources
# | where type == 'microsoft.azurearcdata/postgresinstances'
# | extend workers = toint(properties.spec.workers)
# | project name, resourceGroup, location, workers

Arc-enabled PostgreSQL extends Citus-powered distributed PostgreSQL to Kubernetes clusters anywhere. The coordinator-worker architecture allows horizontal scale-out for high-cardinality workloads like IoT telemetry, multi-tenant SaaS, and geospatial analytics via PostGIS. Choose distribution columns carefully — they determine shard co-location and query fan-out. The Arc Data Controller handles lifecycle, metrics upload, and point-in-time backup infrastructure. Applications always connect to the coordinator endpoint; worker topology is fully transparent to PostgreSQL drivers.
